subject: [PATCH v3 0/7] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary:
Gave Acked-by

Reviewer Shakeel Butt suggested moving __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags, which would allow removing the irq param in reclaim code paths and eliminate the need for irqsave.
reviews.raw_body:
If we move __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags then we can remove the irq
param. It is reclaim code path and I don't think additional cost of irqsave
would matter here.

reviews.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch author failed to properly CC relevant THP maintainers and requested that they do so in future patches.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Joannes,

This is at v3 and you've not cc'd the majority of THP people:

MEMORY MANAGEMENT - THP (TRANSPARENT HUGE PAGE)
M:	Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
M:	David Hildenbrand <david@kernel.org>
M:	Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>            <--- (!)
R:	Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
R:	Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com> <---
R:	Liam R. Howlett <Liam.Howlett@oracle.com>
R:	Nico Pache <npache@redhat.com>              <---
R:	Ryan Roberts <ryan.roberts@arm.com>         <---
R:	Dev Jain <dev.jain@arm.com>                 <---
R:	Barry Song <baohua@kernel.org>              <---
R:	Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>           <---

I'd assume it was an oversight but you're an mm sub-maintainer yourself so
I'm a little surprised...

Anyway could you please make sure to do that going forward?

I'd like to rely on lei's file-tracking stuff to catch cases like this, but
I just haven't got new mail tracking working properly in that environment
and it's really too slow.

It sucks to rely on people cc-ing but email development is what it is :)

Thanks, Lorenzo

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
reviews.summary: Author apologized for an overly broad CC list and promised to include everyone going forward.
reviews.raw_body:
I apologize, there was no ill intent here at all. get_maintainers.pl
gave me 27 addresses for this series and I honestly thought that was
too aggrandizing and self-important for what these patches are lol.

So what you're looking at is a (poor) attempt at trimming down the CC
list based on who I had talked to about these patches at the THP
meeting and who I remember had worked on shrinkers & cgroups.

I can CC everybody going forward, not a problem. Personally I don't
mind getting CCd generously, but my understanding is that some people
do...

2026-03-18.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

reviews.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
reviews.summary:
Lorenzo expressed agreement and understanding towards Johannes Weiner's email etiquette concerns, acknowledging the difficulty in finding a balance between keeping relevant people informed and avoiding self-aggrandizing behavior.

The reviewer suggested using get_maintainer.pl --nogit to trim unnecessary recipients from the patch series, and also recommended dropping 'git' maintainers entries unless explicitly necessary.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks, yeah email for development is a complete pain to be honest!

I wouldn't say that the cc list in any way would be perceived as
self-aggrandizing, I mean some of the trivial stuff I've sent to larger cc
lists :P

But I get your point, it's a kind of impossible balancing act :)

---

Yeah I have often sent series where the list felt... egregiously huge, but
I _generally_ treat it as - people are used to getting tonnes of mail - so
some more shouldn't hurt.

I mean in [0] for example I really cringed at sending a 23 patch series
that is super super mm-centric and really just updating how we manage VMA
flags (trying to get away from system word size flags) - to a trillion
people.

TL;DR - anyway, I think get_maintainer.pl --nogit (and trim anyone who
isn't listed as a maintainer or reviewer) is the right balance to take -
maintainers with screwed up email set ups (*cough* naming no names
beginning with L and ending with orenzo) and reviewers who have signed up
for lots of mail I think are totally fine to always send to.

And of course +cc anybody else you feel appropriate.

The 'git' maintainers entries can absolutely be dropped though, unless
you're explicitly say radically changing something somebody contributed and
it makes sense to include them though all that's subjective!

Cheers, Lorenzo

[0]:https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/cover.1773846935.git.ljs@kernel.org/

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) suggested dropping Reviewers of subsystems for simple function renames, as it helps reduce CC lists on large series
reviews.raw_body:
On some simplistic changes (e.g., simple function rename) I sometimes
drop the Reviewers of subsystems because the simplistic changes are
mostly just as information to maintainers that there might be conflicts.

That helped in the past to reduce the CC lists on series that touch many
archs / subsystems.

-- 
Cheers,

David

reviews.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
reviews.summary: reviewer suggested improving the patch submission process, specifically requesting more functionality from get_maintainer.pl to simplify maintenance tasks
reviews.raw_body:
It'd be nice if get_maintainer.pl could have some functionality to make all this
easier. But that sounds like self-volunteering a bit too much :P *cough*

2026-03-19.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged Shakeel Butt's concern about the workingset shrinker's isolation function using unlock_irq() and cond_resched(), but argued that rewriting it would be non-trivial and introduce a higher maintenance burden.
reviews.raw_body:
The workingset shrinker's isolation function uses unlock_irq() and
cond_resched(). That would be non-trivial to rewrite - pass flags
around; keep irqs disabled for the whole reclaim cycle; break it into
a two-stage process. This sounds like a higher maintenance burden than
the bool here.

I know there is some cost to this distinction, but I actually do find
it useful to know the difference. It's self-documenting context.

2026-03-20.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.
